## Critical Analysis of Traditional Financial Models

Traditional financial models remain foundational to both academic finance and
practical portfolio management. They provide formal definitions of risk and
return, establish benchmark relationships between expected returns and
systematic risk, and support analytical tools for portfolio construction,
performance attribution, and capital allocation. For these reasons, they should
not be treated as obsolete. However, their usefulness depends critically on the
context in which they are applied and on the assumptions under which their
results are interpreted.

The central limitation of many classical models is not that they are internally
incoherent, but that they are often applied as if their assumptions were
empirically stable. Financial markets are adaptive, institutionally constrained,
and subject to structural breaks. Parameters such as expected returns,
correlations, volatility, and liquidity conditions vary over time and may change
abruptly during crises or regime transitions. As a result, models calibrated on
historical samples can exhibit a substantial decline in predictive or
prescriptive quality when deployed out-of-sample.

A second limitation concerns the role of estimation error. In practical
portfolio optimization, small errors in expected return or covariance estimates
can produce disproportionately large changes in optimal weights. This issue is
especially pronounced in high-dimensional settings and in markets with noisy
signals. Consequently, an analytically elegant model may produce unstable or
economically implausible portfolios unless regularization, robust estimation, or
practical constraints are introduced.

A third limitation is the frequent abstraction from market frictions. Many
classical formulations assume frictionless trading, continuous divisibility,
unlimited liquidity at observed prices, and negligible execution costs. These
assumptions can be acceptable for theoretical exposition, but they are
problematic in the design of implementable trading systems. Transaction costs,
slippage, borrow constraints, and market impact can materially alter the
feasibility of a strategy and often determine whether an apparent statistical
edge is economically meaningful.

These limitations are particularly relevant for this thesis, which examines
adaptive trading methods. Reinforcement learning and other modern data-driven
approaches are often motivated precisely by the desire to operate in dynamic,
non-linear, and frictional environments. Nonetheless, the appropriate
methodological stance is not to discard traditional models, but to use them as
benchmarks, diagnostic tools, and conceptual anchors. A critical analysis of
traditional models therefore serves two purposes: it clarifies their domains of
validity and it motivates the need for adaptive methods without overstating
their shortcomings.

### Criticism of the Efficient Market Hypothesis and Modern Portfolio Theory

The Efficient Market Hypothesis (EMH) and Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT) are
among the most influential pillars of modern financial economics. EMH provides
a theory of informational efficiency, while MPT offers a normative framework for
portfolio selection under uncertainty. Their historical significance is
undisputed; however, both are frequently criticized when interpreted as literal
descriptions of market behavior rather than stylized analytical frameworks.

EMH, in its weak, semi-strong, and strong forms, asserts that asset prices
incorporate available information to such an extent that persistent abnormal
profits should not be attainable after adjusting for risk and costs. A major
criticism of this view is empirical: numerous studies document anomalies such as
momentum, value effects, volatility-related effects, and calendar patterns.
Although some anomalies may be partly explained by risk compensation, data
mining, or implementation frictions, their repeated observation challenges a
strict interpretation of universal and instantaneous efficiency.

Another criticism of EMH concerns the institutional and behavioral assumptions
implicit in many applications. Information may not diffuse instantaneously;
agents face capital constraints, transaction costs, regulatory limits, and
heterogeneous objectives. In addition, behavioral finance documents systematic
departures from fully rational updating, including overreaction,
underreaction, framing effects, and herding behavior. These mechanisms do not
imply that markets are easy to beat, but they do suggest that efficiency should
be treated as a matter of degree and market regime, rather than as an absolute
state.

MPT faces a different, though related, set of criticisms. In its standard
mean-variance formulation, portfolio choice is based on expected returns,
variances, and covariances. This approach is elegant and analytically powerful,
but highly sensitive to estimation error. In empirical applications, expected
returns are notoriously noisy, and small estimation changes can produce
substantial shifts in optimized portfolio weights. As a result, unconstrained
mean-variance portfolios often exhibit extreme allocations and poor out-of-sample
stability.

A further criticism concerns the treatment of risk. Variance penalizes upside
and downside fluctuations symmetrically, which may be inappropriate for
investors primarily concerned with drawdowns and tail losses. Realized returns
also exhibit skewness, kurtosis, and volatility clustering, which are not well
captured by Gaussian assumptions frequently used in simplified expositions of
MPT. In practice, this has led to the widespread use of alternative risk
measures and robust portfolio optimization techniques.

MPT is also commonly criticized for its frictionless assumptions. Basic
formulations omit transaction costs, taxes, bid-ask spreads, market impact, and
rebalancing constraints. These omissions can materially distort the practical
performance of optimized portfolios, especially in high-turnover strategies. In
algorithmic trading contexts, where execution quality and cost control are
central determinants of performance, such assumptions are particularly limiting.

Despite these criticisms, EMH and MPT remain valuable. EMH provides a rigorous
benchmark that cautions against naive claims of persistent alpha, while MPT
establishes a coherent language for diversification and risk-return trade-offs.
The appropriate conclusion is therefore not rejection, but qualified use:
traditional models are indispensable as theoretical baselines and diagnostic
tools, yet they are insufficient on their own for modelling adaptive,
non-stationary, and frictional trading environments. This tension motivates the
investigation of machine learning and reinforcement learning methods in later
chapters of the thesis.
